title: Power forward (basketball)

infobox.value: |  |  | | --- | --- | | 1 (Floor General) | * [Point guard](/wiki/Point_guard "Point guard") (PG) * [Combo guard](/wiki/Combo_guard "Combo guard") (PG/SG) | | 2 (Wing) | * [Shooting guard](/wiki/Shooting_guard "Shooting guard") (SG) * Guard-forward / [Swingman](/wiki/Swingman "Swingman") (GF , SG/SF or SG/PF) | | [An overview of a basketball court where players would be positions](/wiki/File:Basketball_positions.svg "Basketball half-court")

|  |  | | --- | --- | | 3 (Wing) | * [Small forward](/wiki/Small_forward "Small forward") (SF) * [Point forward](/wiki/Point_forward "Point forward") (PG/SF or PG/PF) | | 4 (Post) | * Power forward (PF) * Combo forward / [Stretch four](/wiki/Stretch_four "Stretch four") (SF/PF) |

(**PF**), also known as the **four**, is one of the five traditional [positions](/wiki/Basketball_positions "Basketball positions") in a regulation [basketball](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball") game. Traditionally, power forwards have played a role similar to [centers](/wiki/Center_(basketball) "Center (basketball)") and are typically the tallest or second tallest player on the court. They are also usually leaner than centers. During an offensive possession, the power forward typically plays with their backs toward the basket and are typically a "go-to" position in regard to scoring in the [post](/wiki/Post_(basketball) "Post (basketball)"). In a [pick and roll](/wiki/Pick_and_roll "Pick and roll") offense, the power forward typically sets a screen for a guard and "rolls" towards the basket to receive a pass (or "pops" towards the perimeter for an open shot). When on [defense](/wiki/Defense_(sport) "Defense (sport)"), they typically position themselves under the basket in a [zone defense](/wiki/Zone_defense "Zone defense") or against the opposing power forward in [man-to-man defense](/wiki/Man-to-man_defense "Man-to-man defense").[[5]](#cite_note-5) The power forward position entails a variety of responsibilities, including [rebounding](/wiki/Rebound_(basketball) "Rebound (basketball)"), [screen](/wiki/Screen_(sports) "Screen (sports)") setting, [rim protecting](/wiki/Block_(basketball) "Block (basketball)"), and scoring.[[6]](#cite_note-6)
["ESPN.com's Greatest Power Forwards"](https://www.espn.com/nba/news/story?id=2074360). *ESPN*. ESPN Internet Ventures. June 2, 2005. Retrieved December 11, 2023.

Rosen, Charley (July 18, 2005). ["Best all-time power forwards"](https://web.archive.org/web/20050719002301/http://msn.foxsports.com/nba/story/3747894). *Fox Sports*. MSN. Archived from [the original](http://msn.foxsports.com/nba/story/3747894) on July 19, 2005. Retrieved January 31, 2008.

DuPree, David (June 7, 2007). ["Tim Duncan: Best power forward ever?"](https://www.usatoday.com/sports/basketball/nba/spurs/2007-06-06-bonus-duncan_N.htm). *USA Today*. Gannett Company. Retrieved June 17, 2007.
["Who are the top five power forwards in the WNBA?"](https://www.espn.com/nba/news/story?id=2074360). *ESPN*. June 28, 2018. Retrieved June 3, 2024.

["NBA.com - Players and Positions"](https://web.archive.org/web/20021001084016/http://www.nba.com/canada/Basketball_U_Players_and_Posi-Canada_Generic_Article-18037.html). *NBA.com*. Archived from [the original](http://www.nba.com/canada/Basketball_U_Players_and_Posi-Canada_Generic_Article-18037.html) on October 1, 2002.
["How to Play Power Forward in Basketball"](https://www.hoopsaddict.com/how-to-play-power-forward-in-basketball/#What_is_the_Role_of_a_Power_Forward_in_Basketball). *Hoops Addict*. 25 May 2022.

Burns, Scott (January 11, 2013). ["Ranking the NBA's Best "Tweeners""](https://bleacherreport.com/articles/1480043-ranking-the-nbas-best-tweeners). [Bleacher Report](/wiki/Bleacher_Report "Bleacher Report"). Retrieved March 26, 2020.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Power forward (basketball)](/w/index.php?title=Power_forward_(basketball)&redirect=no "Power forward (basketball)"))

Position in the sport of basketball

For other uses, see [Power forward (disambiguation)](/wiki/Power_forward_(disambiguation) "Power forward (disambiguation)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/Tim_Duncan_Walks_Verizon_Center%27s_Floor_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Tim_Duncan_Walks_Verizon_Center%27s_Floor_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tim_Duncan_Walks_Verizon_Center%27s_Floor_(cropped).jpg)

[Tim Duncan](/wiki/Tim_Duncan "Tim Duncan") is often regarded as one of the greatest power forwards in the history of the [National Basketball Association](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association") (NBA).[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/BreannaStewart_foulshot_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-BreannaStewart_foulshot_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:BreannaStewart_foulshot_(cropped).jpg)

[Breanna Stewart](/wiki/Breanna_Stewart "Breanna Stewart") is regarded as a top power forward in the [Women's National Basketball Association](/wiki/Women%27s_National_Basketball_Association "Women's National Basketball Association") (WNBA).[[4]](#cite_note-4)

The **power forward** (**PF**), also known as the **four**, is one of the five traditional [positions](/wiki/Basketball_positions "Basketball positions") in a regulation [basketball](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball") game. Traditionally, power forwards have played a role similar to [centers](/wiki/Center_(basketball) "Center (basketball)") and are typically the tallest or second tallest player on the court. They are also usually leaner than centers. During an offensive possession, the power forward typically plays with their backs toward the basket and are typically a "go-to" position in regard to scoring in the [post](/wiki/Post_(basketball) "Post (basketball)"). In a [pick and roll](/wiki/Pick_and_roll "Pick and roll") offense, the power forward typically sets a screen for a guard and "rolls" towards the basket to receive a pass (or "pops" towards the perimeter for an open shot). When on [defense](/wiki/Defense_(sport) "Defense (sport)"), they typically position themselves under the basket in a [zone defense](/wiki/Zone_defense "Zone defense") or against the opposing power forward in [man-to-man defense](/wiki/Man-to-man_defense "Man-to-man defense").[[5]](#cite_note-5) The power forward position entails a variety of responsibilities, including [rebounding](/wiki/Rebound_(basketball) "Rebound (basketball)"), [screen](/wiki/Screen_(sports) "Screen (sports)") setting, [rim protecting](/wiki/Block_(basketball) "Block (basketball)"), and scoring.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Many power forwards are noted for their mid-range jump-shot, and several players have become very accurate from 12 to 18 feet (3.7 to 5.5 m). Earlier, these skills were more usually exhibited in the European style of play. Some power forwards, known as [stretch fours](/wiki/Stretch_four "Stretch four"), have since extended their shooting range to include [three-point field goals](/wiki/Three-point_field_goal "Three-point field goal").[[7]](#cite_note-:0-7)

In the [NBA](/wiki/NBA "NBA"), power forwards usually range from 6' 9" (2.06 m) to 7' 0" (2.13 m) while in the [WNBA](/wiki/WNBA "WNBA"), power forwards are usually between 6' 0" (1.83 m) and 6' 3" (1.91 m). Despite the averages, a variety of players fit "[tweener](/wiki/Tweener_(basketball) "Tweener (basketball)")" roles which finds them in the [small forward](/wiki/Small_forward "Small forward") or center position depending on matchups and coaching decisions.[[8]](#cite_note-8) Some power forwards, such as [Draymond Green](/wiki/Draymond_Green "Draymond Green") and even 6' 5" (1.96 m) [P. J. Tucker](/wiki/P._J._Tucker "P. J. Tucker"), have played at the [center](/wiki/Center_(basketball) "Center (basketball)") position, possessing the skills, but lacking the height that is usually associated with the position.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Power forwards who have been inducted in the [Naismith Memorial Basketball Hall of Fame](/wiki/Naismith_Memorial_Basketball_Hall_of_Fame "Naismith Memorial Basketball Hall of Fame") include [Karl Malone](/wiki/Karl_Malone "Karl Malone"), [Lauren Jackson](/wiki/Lauren_Jackson "Lauren Jackson"), [Vern Mikkelsen](/wiki/Vern_Mikkelsen "Vern Mikkelsen"), [Tina Thompson](/wiki/Tina_Thompson "Tina Thompson"), [Dirk Nowitzki](/wiki/Dirk_Nowitzki "Dirk Nowitzki"), [Chris Webber](/wiki/Chris_Webber "Chris Webber"), [Kevin Garnett](/wiki/Kevin_Garnett "Kevin Garnett"), [Dolph Schayes](/wiki/Dolph_Schayes "Dolph Schayes"), [Kevin McHale](/wiki/Kevin_McHale_(basketball) "Kevin McHale (basketball)"), [Charles Barkley](/wiki/Charles_Barkley "Charles Barkley"), [Dennis Rodman](/wiki/Dennis_Rodman "Dennis Rodman"), [Elvin Hayes](/wiki/Elvin_Hayes "Elvin Hayes"), [Bob Pettit](/wiki/Bob_Pettit "Bob Pettit"), [Jerry Lucas](/wiki/Jerry_Lucas "Jerry Lucas"), [Dave Debusschere](/wiki/Dave_Debusschere "Dave Debusschere"), and [Tim Duncan](/wiki/Tim_Duncan "Tim Duncan").

## The "stretch four"

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Power_forward&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: The \"stretch four\"")]

See also: [Stretch four](/wiki/Stretch_four "Stretch four")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/Robert_Horry_2012_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Robert_Horry_2012_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Robert_Horry_2012_(cropped).jpg)

Power forward [Robert Horry](/wiki/Robert_Horry "Robert Horry") innovated the power forward position by pioneering the "stretch four" shooter.

Some power forwards are noted for their perimeter shooting, both from the mid-range and three-point distance. Since the 2000s, several power forwards have become very accurate from 12 to 18 feet (3.7 to 5.5 m). Earlier, these skills were more typically exhibited in the European style of play. Some power forwards, known as [stretch fours](/wiki/Stretch_four "Stretch four"), have since extended their shooting range to include [three-point field goals](/wiki/Three-point_field_goal "Three-point field goal").[[7]](#cite_note-:0-7) Some stretch fours utilize their mid-range scoring in the post. [Dallas Mavericks](/wiki/Dallas_Mavericks "Dallas Mavericks") franchise player [Dirk Nowitzki](/wiki/Dirk_Nowitzki "Dirk Nowitzki") utilized the [fadeaway](/wiki/Fadeaway "Fadeaway") shot to great effect. Stretch fours have also proven to be reliable in certain pick and roll designs, in which the power forward "pops" away from the basket for an open shot if the guard is being doubled while moving off of a screen. 6'10" (2.08m) power forward [Amar'e Stoudemire](/wiki/Amar%27e_Stoudemire "Amar'e Stoudemire") utilized the "pick and pop" play to great effect while playing with [Steve Nash](/wiki/Steve_Nash "Steve Nash") on the [Phoenix Suns](/wiki/Phoenix_Suns "Phoenix Suns") from 2004 to 2010. Notable examples of stretch fours include [Jayson Tatum](/wiki/Jayson_Tatum "Jayson Tatum"), [Robert Horry](/wiki/Robert_Horry "Robert Horry"), [Al Horford](/wiki/Al_Horford "Al Horford"), [Rasheed Wallace](/wiki/Rasheed_Wallace "Rasheed Wallace"), Amar'e Stoudemire, Dirk Nowitzki, [Carmelo Anthony](/wiki/Carmelo_Anthony "Carmelo Anthony"), [Kevin Love](/wiki/Kevin_Love "Kevin Love"), and [Steve Novak](/wiki/Steve_Novak "Steve Novak").
